hint: Sacramento trio known for abrasive, confrontational music with MC Ride's aggressive delivery
explanation: Experimental hip-hop (also called noise rap or industrial hip-hop) — Death Grips fuse hip-hop structure with industrial noise, hardcore punk aggression, and avant-garde electronics. Distinct from trap metal (e.g. $uicideboy$) which is moodier, or cloud rap which is ambient and spacey.

hint: 28 minutes, 10 tracks, considered one of the most extreme records of its era
explanation: Thrash metal — Slayer are one of the 'Big Four' of thrash (with Metallica, Megadeth, Anthrax). Reign in Blood pushed thrash to its extreme edge, but it's still classifiable as thrash. Death metal came shortly after and features more guttural vocals and technical riffing. Speed metal is an earlier, slightly less aggressive predecessor.

hint: Features a sample of Popcaan, prominent bassline, one-drop rhythm
explanation: Dancehall-influenced R&B — Controlla draws heavily from Jamaican dancehall (the one-drop rhythm, Popcaan sample, patois inflections) but sits within a pop R&B framework. Distinct from reggaeton (Latin, originated in Puerto Rico) or Afrobeats (West African-rooted).

hint: Anonymous South London producer, debut 2006, textures of rain and broken urban environments
explanation: Post-dubstep — Burial uses dubstep's bass-weight and South London atmosphere but strips away the aggression and 'wub' in favour of spectral, melancholic textures. Classic dubstep (Skream, Benga) is harder and more dance-floor oriented.

hint: Massachusetts band active since 1990, known for chaotic guitar work and screamed vocals
explanation: Metalcore — though Converge blur lines, they're foundational metalcore: metal riffing combined with hardcore punk intensity. Mathcore (The Dillinger Escape Plan) is more rhythmically chaotic and technical. Screamo (Saetia, pg.99) is more rooted in emo and is softer. Grindcore (Napalm Death) is faster and shorter-form.

hint: Belfast duo, widely considered a modern peak of a specific British electronic tradition
explanation: Acid house — Glue is built around that signature Roland TB-303 bassline squelch that defines acid house. Deep house is warmer and more chord-based. UK garage has a shuffled 2-step rhythm. Bicep are deep lovers of the original late-80s acid house era and this track is a direct tribute to it.

hint: Swedish artist, breakout 2005–2010 era, often described as emotional but undeniably club-ready
explanation: Electropop — Robyn sits right at the intersection of electropop and dance-pop: electronic production with pop songwriting crafted for the club. Synthpop (Depeche Mode, New Order) is an earlier 80s-rooted category. Nu-disco leans into 70s funk-tinged disco revivals. Dance-pop is a related but broader label that undersells the electronic emphasis here.

hint: Live instrumentation, jazz, funk, spoken word, politically charged, Compton roots
explanation: Jazz rap — TPAB is saturated in West Coast jazz tradition (Flying Lotus, Thundercat), Parliament-Funkadelic-era funk, and lyrically self-aware conscious hip-hop. G-funk is a West Coast subgenre with a very specific synthesizer sound. Trap uses 808s and hi-hats. TPAB deliberately rejects those sounds. The genre is also called conscious hip-hop, but jazz rap best captures the sonic identity.

hint: Dublin/London band, album cost a rumoured £250,000, guitar tone nicknamed 'The Glide'
explanation: Shoegaze — MBV are arguably THE shoegaze band. The genre is named after performers staring at their pedal boards on stage. Heavy use of reverb, tremolo, and feedback to create walls of sound while retaining pop melody. Dream pop is similar but cleaner and less distorted. Noise pop adds more abrasive punk energy.

hint: Shepherd's Bush rapper, melodic delivery over a Spanish guitar-tinged beat
explanation: UK drill — Central Cee is one of UK drill's biggest names. UK drill evolved from Chicago drill but adapted to South London, using slower tempos (~140 BPM), syncopated hi-hats, and dark minor-key melodies. Grime (Dizzee Rascal, Wiley) is faster and predates drill. Afro-swing blends R&B with Afrobeats influence.

hint: Lagos bandleader, 70s–80s, 20-minute tracks, brass sections, politically charged Yoruba-inflected lyrics
explanation: Afrobeat — note the spelling: no 's'. Fela Kuti literally invented Afrobeat by fusing James Brown-style funk with West African highlife and jazz. Modern Afrobeats (with an 's') is a completely different, contemporary genre — pop-oriented, digitally produced, associated with Burna Boy, Wizkid. The two are often confused but span 50 years of distance.

hint: Atlanta duo, live instrumentation, André 3000's eccentricity vs Big Boi's street grounding, Dungeon Family
explanation: Southern hip-hop, specifically rooted in the Atlanta Dungeon Family collective. Aquemini resists easy labelling — it's too organic and eclectic for G-funk (LA and synth-heavy), too hip-hop for neo-soul, and predates and is far more refined than crunk. The Dungeon Family lineage is central to understanding OutKast's sound.

hint: Birmingham MC Mike Skinner, debut 2002, everyday London/Brum life, spoken-word cadences
explanation: UK garage — The Streets grew out of the UK garage scene (2-step, speed garage) but Skinner's conversational, wordy delivery and kitchen-sink subject matter made it something sui generis. Often filed under British hip-hop but that undersells the garage DNA. The music is grime-adjacent — grime had barely been named when Original Pirate Material dropped — but the rhythmic foundation is UK garage.

hint: Rio de Janeiro, samba percussion, social commentary, favela perspective, gentle melodic guitar
explanation: Samba-soul — Seu Jorge blends traditional samba rhythm with soul and funk production, a style rooted in Black Rio, the 1970s movement that fused American soul with Brazilian samba. Sometimes called samba funk when the funk influence is more pronounced. Bossa nova is cooler, jazzier, and upper-middle-class in origin. Tropicália (Caetano Veloso, Os Mutantes) is 60s avant-garde. MPB is an umbrella term, too broad.

hint: Claimed to be from Saturn, big band, avant-garde, cosmic philosophy, active 1950s–1990s
explanation: Free jazz — Sun Ra sits in the free jazz and avant-garde tradition. Hard bop (Art Blakey, Horace Silver) is rhythmically grounded blues-and-gospel-inflected bebop. Cool jazz (Miles Davis's Birth of the Cool) is restrained and cerebral. Modal jazz (Kind of Blue) uses scales rather than chord changes. Sun Ra rejected all conventions — free jazz best captures his boundary-dissolving approach.

hint: Deejay style, fast-paced, digital riddim, one of the defining recordings of its moment
explanation: Dancehall — specifically early digital dancehall (also called 'sleng teng era' after the Wayne Smith/King Jammy track that same year). Roots reggae (Bob Marley, Burning Spear) is slower, Rastafari-influenced, live band. Ska is the 1960s predecessor — faster tempo, offbeat horns. Dub strips songs down to bass and drum with heavy FX manipulation.

hint: Düsseldorf, founded 1970, drum machines, vocoders, 'man-machine' aesthetic
explanation: Krautrock — Kraftwerk began in the krautrock scene (experimental German rock of the early 70s) before inventing their own stripped electronic sound. They're the godparents of synthpop, techno, hip-hop, and electro — but none of those genres had been named when they made their most important records. Synthpop (Depeche Mode) descends from them; EBM (Front 242) is harsher industrial-electronic.

hint: Bristol, debut album, cinematic samples, turntablism, female vocals drenched in reverb
explanation: Trip-hop — Portishead alongside Massive Attack and Tricky defined trip-hop: Bristol-born, hip-hop-rooted but slower, darker, sample-heavy, cinematic. Downtempo is a broader, more neutral electronic category. Dream pop is guitar-based and less urban. Darkwave is goth-adjacent electronic with cold synths. Trip-hop has a very specific mid-90s Bristol fingerprint.

hint: Kristin Hayter, classically trained, extreme vocals, themes of abuse and religion, piano and noise
explanation: Neoclassical darkwave — Lingua Ignota defies easy categorisation but combines operatic classical training with extreme metal intensity and dark ambient texture. The music is also described as extreme music for its vocal violence and emotional weight. Black metal is a stretch — the metal elements appear but it's not riff-based. Dark cabaret (Amanda Palmer, Dresden Dolls) is theatrical but far less aggressive. Post-industrial is closer but misses the classical grandeur.

hint: Barranquilla, accordion, cumbia-inflected rhythm, modernised coastal Colombian sound, ft. Shakira
explanation: Vallenato-pop — Vives has spent his career modernising vallenato, a Colombian folk genre from the Caribbean coast built around accordion, caja drum, and guacharaca. La Bicicleta is a polished pop production but the vallenato DNA is front and centre. Cumbia is a related coastal Colombian genre but with different rhythm and instrumentation. Reggaeton uses dembow rhythm, which is absent here.

hint: Umeå band, polymetric riffs, mechanical precision, 7-minute single-riff endurance test
explanation: Djent — Meshuggah are the architects of djent: polyrhythmic, palm-muted low-tuned guitar patterns with a distinctive percussive tone. The genre sits within progressive metal, but djent is the precise label. Technical death metal (Necrophagist) focuses on speed and complexity differently. Industrial metal (Fear Factory) uses electronics. Mathcore (Dillinger) is more chaotic and less groovy.

hint: Tuareg musicians from the Sahara desert, electric guitars meeting ancient Tamashek vocal tradition
explanation: Desert blues — also called Tishoumaren — Tinariwen pioneered this genre fusing Tuareg folk music with electric blues and rock guitar. It's distinct from Afrobeat (West African funk-jazz), Gnawa (Moroccan trance music), and Ethio-jazz (Ethiopian jazz fusion). The hypnotic, cyclical guitar patterns and call-and-response vocals are hallmarks.

hint: Catalan artist, handclaps (palmas), melismatic singing, 808 bass, flamenco structures with electronic production
explanation: Neo-flamenco — Rosalía's breakthrough fuses traditional flamenco vocal technique (melisma, quejío) and rhythmic patterns (compás) with contemporary electronic production. Sometimes called flamenco-pop for its accessibility, but the production is too experimental for that label. Reggaeton uses dembow rhythm, which isn't present here. Latin trap is hip-hop-derived. Rumba catalana is street-level, acoustic, and less experimental.

hint: Cornwall-born Richard D. James, Warp Records, complex breakbeats, unsettling but beautiful electronics
explanation: IDM (Intelligent Dance Music) — Aphex Twin is synonymous with the genre (though he dislikes the name). Windowlicker layers intricate programmed beats with lush synth melodies. Drum and bass is faster and more dancefloor-oriented. Ambient techno (his own Selected Ambient Works) is more atmospheric. Breakcore (Venetian Snares) is more extreme and chaotic.

hint: Biggest Latin artist of the streaming era, dembow rhythm, bilingual lyrics, Jhay Cortez feature
explanation: Reggaeton — Dakiti is built on the classic dembow riddim (boom-ch-boom-chick pattern) that defines reggaeton. Latin trap uses trap hi-hats and 808s without the dembow. Dominican dembow is faster and rawer. Moombahton is a hybrid of Dutch house and reggaeton at a slower tempo. Bad Bunny has moved between all these but Dakiti is pure reggaeton.

album: Lift Your Skinny Fists Like Antennas to Heaven
hint: Montreal collective, no vocals, 20-minute movements, field recordings, crescendo-driven orchestration
explanation: Post-rock — GY!BE are central to post-rock: long-form instrumental pieces that use rock instruments to create orchestral dynamics. The genre builds slowly from quiet to loud. Ambient (Brian Eno) is more static. Drone metal (Sunn O)))) is heavier and more repetitive. Shoegaze has pop song structures and vocals.

hint: Port Harcourt artist, Toni Braxton sample, danceable, global crossover hit, Grammy winner
explanation: Afrobeats (with an 's') — the contemporary West African pop sound. Distinct from Fela Kuti's Afrobeat (no 's'), which is jazz-funk-driven. Afrobeats is digitally produced, pop-oriented, and globally streamed. Amapiano is a South African genre with a different rhythmic feel. Last Last's production, tempo, and structure are pure Afrobeats.

hint: San Francisco, blast beats combined with shimmering major-key guitar, pink album cover, divisive in metal circles
explanation: Blackgaze — Deafheaven pioneered the fusion of black metal's blast beats and shrieked vocals with shoegaze's dreamy, reverb-drenched guitar textures. Pure black metal (Darkthrone) is rawer and darker. Post-metal (Isis, Neurosis) is slower and sludgier. Shoegaze (MBV) lacks the metal intensity entirely.

hint: Addis Ababa, vibraphone and organ, pentatonic scales, featured in Broken Flowers soundtrack
explanation: Ethio-jazz — Mulatu Astatke is the father of Ethiopian jazz, which fuses traditional Ethiopian pentatonic scales and rhythms with American jazz, Latin percussion, and funk. Afrobeat is West African. Bossa nova is Brazilian. Exotica is a 1950s American genre that superficially uses 'exotic' sounds but lacks authenticity.

hint: Bow, East London, debut at age 18, aggressive MC delivery over angular electronic beats, 140 BPM
explanation: Grime — Dizzee Rascal's Boy in da Corner is one of grime's founding documents. Grime emerged from UK garage, speeding up to ~140 BPM with aggressive MC delivery and angular synth stabs. UK garage is smoother and more soulful. UK drill came a decade later with different rhythmic patterns. Dubstep is instrumental and bass-heavy.

hint: Reykjavik band, bowed guitar, falsetto vocals in Icelandic, cinematic crescendos, glacial beauty
explanation: Post-rock — Sigur Rós create expansive, cinematic instrumental passages (despite having vocals) that build from quiet to overwhelmingly loud. Their approach to dynamics and song structure is quintessentially post-rock. Ambient is more static. Shoegaze is more distorted and guitar-driven. Dream pop has more conventional song structures.

hint: Masked villain persona, Madlib production, off-kilter rhyme schemes, comic-book samples
explanation: Abstract hip-hop — DOOM and Madlib's Madvillainy is a cornerstone of abstract and underground rap: dense wordplay, unconventional flows, obscure samples, and experimental structure. Boom bap (DJ Premier) is more straightforward rhythmically. Jazz rap (A Tribe Called Quest) is jazzier and more accessible. Lo-fi hip-hop is instrumental and more relaxed.

hint: Venezuelan-born, experimental producer, collaborator with Björk and FKA twigs, shapeshifting electronic textures
explanation: Deconstructed club — Arca takes dance music elements (reggaeton, techno, trance) and warps them into something alien and boundary-dissolving. This is also called experimental electronic, but deconstructed club is the more precise term for this post-club, rave-influenced avant-garde. Hyperpop (100 gecs) is poppier and more maximalist. PC Music (A.G. Cook) is more tongue-in-cheek and bubblegum. Vaporwave is nostalgic and sample-based.

hint: Lagos superstar, Tems feature, soft percussion, summer hit that crossed over globally, log drum rhythm
explanation: Afrobeats — Essence is one of the defining Afrobeats tracks of the 2020s. The log drum pattern, mid-tempo groove, and melodic vocal style are hallmarks of modern Afrobeats. Amapiano (South African) has a different bassline and rhythm. Classic Afrobeat (Fela) is live-band funk-jazz. Dancehall (Jamaican) has a different rhythmic structure.

album: You Won't Get What You Want
hint: Providence, RI band, uncomfortable tension, grinding guitars, industrial textures, Birthday Party influence
explanation: Noise rock — Daughters create abrasive, tension-filled music that draws from noise rock (Swans, The Birthday Party) and industrial (Ministry, Godflesh). Industrial textures are strong here, but the song structures and punk aggression place it firmly in noise rock. Post-punk is more structured and less extreme. Sludge metal is heavier and more riff-driven. No wave (DNA, Mars) was a late-70s NYC movement with a similar aesthetic but different era.

hint: Tokyo producer, jazz piano samples, laid-back beats, Cise Starr vocals, study-music icon
explanation: Jazz hip-hop — Nujabes pioneered the fusion of smooth jazz samples with hip-hop beats that became the foundation of the lo-fi hip-hop movement. Boom bap is grittier and New York-rooted. Trip-hop is darker and more cinematic. Neo-soul is more vocal and R&B-oriented. Nujabes' warmth and melodic sensibility set him apart from all of these.

hint: Tuareg guitarist from Agadez, blistering solos, psychedelic wah pedal, Tamasheq lyrics, Saharan rock
explanation: Desert rock — also called Tuareg guitar — Mdou Moctar takes the Tishoumaren tradition and cranks it up with Hendrix-inspired electric guitar heroics. Psychedelic rock is Western and lacks the Saharan melodic vocabulary. Afrobeat is West African funk-jazz. Blues rock shares the guitar intensity but not the rhythmic or tonal framework.

hint: Reykjavik, industrial bass, distorted beats, operatic vocals, post-Sugarcubes solo career
explanation: Art pop — Björk's music defies genre but sits in the art pop tradition: experimental, boundary-pushing music rooted in pop songcraft. Electronic production is central to her work, but art pop is the broader container that holds it together. Trip-hop is Bristol-specific and more hip-hop-rooted. Industrial (Nine Inch Nails) is harder and more mechanical. Synthpop is more conventional and 80s-derived.

hint: Odd Future founder, warm synths, Kali Uchis feature, surprisingly tender and melodic, LA sunlight
explanation: Neo-soul rap — Flower Boy marked Tyler's pivot from abrasive horrorcore to warm, soulful, introspective hip-hop influenced by Pharrell, Stevie Wonder, and bossa nova. It's also called alternative hip-hop for its refusal to follow genre convention, but neo-soul rap best describes the sonic palette. Cloud rap (Yung Lean) is hazier and more detached. Boom bap is grittier. Funk rap (Anderson .Paak) is more rhythmically driven.

hint: Queen of Portuguese music, guitarra portuguesa accompaniment, themes of fate and longing (saudade)
explanation: Fado — Portugal's signature musical form, characterised by the guitarra portuguesa (12-string guitar), melancholic vocals, and themes of saudade (bittersweet longing). Bossa nova is Brazilian and jazzier. Flamenco is Spanish with different rhythms and guitar technique. Chanson is French cabaret-rooted vocal music.

hint: Tottenham MC, minimal beat, aggressive delivery, 'That's not me' era, Mercury Prize winner
explanation: Grime — Skepta is one of grime's central figures. Shutdown's sparse, aggressive beat and rapid-fire delivery are classic grime elements. UK drill came later and has different rhythmic patterns. UK garage is smoother and more soulful. Road rap is a grittier UK hip-hop style without grime's electronic production DNA.

hint: Grangemouth, Scotland, Elizabeth Fraser's ethereal glossolalia vocals, 4AD label, layered guitars
explanation: Dream pop — Cocteau Twins defined dream pop: lush, atmospheric guitar textures with angelic, often indecipherable vocals creating a dreamy, otherworldly mood. Shoegaze (MBV) is heavier and more distorted. Ethereal wave (Dead Can Dance) leans more gothic. Ambient pop is more minimal and less guitar-driven.

hint: Faisalabad, powerful Sufi devotional singing, produced by Michael Brook, tablas and harmonium with ambient textures
explanation: Qawwali — Nusrat is the master of qawwali, the Sufi devotional vocal tradition. Mustt Mustt is a fusion version: traditional qawwali with Western ambient production by Michael Brook. Hindustani classical is instrumental and raga-based. Bhangra is Punjabi dance music. Ghazal is Urdu/Persian poetic song, more restrained and less percussive than qawwali's ecstatic repetition.

hint: Bahia artist, psychedelic guitars, avant-garde Brazilian pop, political movement against military dictatorship
explanation: Tropicália — a late-1960s Brazilian movement that fused traditional Brazilian music with psychedelic rock, avant-garde experimentation, and political protest. Bossa nova is the cooler, jazzier predecessor. MPB (Música Popular Brasileira) is the broader umbrella that Tropicália helped create. Psychedelic rock shares influences but Tropicália is distinctly Brazilian.

hint: West Berlin, instruments built from scrap metal and power tools, Blixa Bargeld's vocals, Teutonic extremity
explanation: Industrial — Einstürzende Neubauten are foundational industrial: music created from non-traditional instruments (metal sheets, drills, concrete) as artistic statement. Noise rock (Sonic Youth) uses conventional instruments unconventionally. Krautrock (Can, Faust) is 70s experimental. EBM (Front 242, DAF) is dance-oriented industrial with drum machines and synth sequences.

hint: Surrey brothers, Sam Smith vocals, two-step rhythm, bassline driven, UK club music
explanation: UK garage revival — Disclosure brought back the two-step shuffled rhythm and bassline-driven sound of late-90s UK garage, updated with modern production. Sometimes called future garage for its cleaner, contemporary feel. Deep house is four-on-the-floor and more mellow. Nu-disco revives 70s/80s disco. Bass music is a broad umbrella. Latch's syncopated rhythm is pure UK garage DNA.

hint: Dakar, griot tradition, Neneh Cherry duet, tama drum, global pop crossover from West African roots
explanation: Mbalax — Youssou N'Dour's music is rooted in mbalax, Senegal's national popular music built on sabar drum patterns. 7 Seconds is a polished Afro-pop crossover version of that sound. Afrobeat (Fela) is Nigerian funk-jazz. Soukous is Congolese guitar-driven dance music. Highlife is Ghanaian/Nigerian and has a different melodic sensibility.

hint: Tokyo trio, feedback worship, massive guitar volume, shifts from delicate to earth-shaking, Wata's soaring lead
explanation: Stoner metal — Boris blur lines between drone, stoner, and noise but Pink is primarily stoner-doom: heavy, fuzz-drenched riffs at moderate tempos with psychedelic textures. The drone influence is strong, but the riff-centric structure and distortion levels put it in stoner metal territory. Doom metal (Black Sabbath) is slower and more riff-focused. Post-rock is less heavy and more dynamic. Sludge metal (Eyehategod) is more aggressive and punk-influenced.

hint: Wassoulou region, kamale ngoni lute, pentatonic melodies, songs about women's rights, warm vocal style
explanation: Wassoulou music — a genre from southern Mali built around the kamale ngoni (hunter's harp), pentatonic scales, and didadi rhythm. Distinct from Manding griot music (which uses kora and balafon in the Manding tradition). Highlife is coastal West African (Ghana/Nigeria). Desert blues is Tuareg and northern Saharan.

hint: Manchester, Martin Hannett production, cavernous drums, Ian Curtis baritone, Factory Records
explanation: Post-punk — Joy Division are foundational post-punk: deconstructing punk's energy with atmospheric production, introspective lyrics, and experimental song structures. Goth rock (Bauhaus, Sisters of Mercy) is a descendant with more theatrical elements. New wave is poppier and more synth-driven. Coldwave is French post-punk with a slightly different aesthetic.
